questions.text: I believe that the measure of a good person is how seriously they take the suffering of others.
I am happy to row in sync with everyone else rather than steer alone.
When something delights me, the delight fills me completely.
I hold the view that the strongest contribution I can make starts with a strong self.
I find that my effectiveness in demanding situations is preserved by my ability to process emotional content without internalizing it.
I feel that taking charge is a natural extension of caring about results.
I believe that strong personal conviction is necessary for effective leadership.
I detect when someone remembers a detail about you not out of genuine care but to demonstrate attentiveness.
I find that I can recall difficult experiences without the associated feelings being reactivated.
I notice when an email is bcc'd to someone based on the overly formal tone.
I prefer that timelines be negotiated among everyone involved.
I believe that a measured, analytical response to suffering is often more helpful than an emotionally engaged one.
My emotional state is like a lake with a deep bottom — disturbances on the surface don't reach down very far.
I dive into collaborative work and let expectations clarify themselves through action.
I find that an unresolved disagreement keeps my nervous system activated like a low-level alarm.
I find that my natural temperament allows me to stay composed when others might lose their footing.
I tend to exit commitments that no longer align with where I want to go personally.
I often notice when someone's enthusiasm is performed rather than felt.
I routinely decline requests that would compromise my personal goals.
I do not naturally notice when a conversation between two people nearby is meant for me to overhear.
I find that the felt weight of others' suffering is the moral reality that keeps me engaged with the world rather than retreating into private concerns.
When I feel rejected, the pain is immediate, physical, and consuming.
I draft and revise important emails before sending them.
I approach problems by first defining them clearly, then generating solutions systematically.
I can assess a suffering person's situation clearly precisely because I am not emotionally affected by it.
Witnessing others' suffering makes it impossible for me to remain indifferent.
I find that thorough preparation gives me an edge in almost every situation.
I believe the group should hold each other accountable rather than one person doing it.
I trust my sense of direction during complex work without stopping to verify at fixed points.
I tend to put others' comfort ahead of efficiency when the two conflict.
I tend not to register when someone is slowly distancing themselves from a relationship.
I believe that a leader who hesitates loses credibility.
I choose novelty over reliability because trying new things is how I learn.
I often do not notice when someone feels excluded from a group.
I would happily take a pay cut if it meant funding a cause I care about.
I would rather mentor someone else's talent than develop a new skill of my own.
